title: Cereal yield vs. share in extreme poverty
subtitle: Extreme poverty is defined as living below the [International Poverty Line](#dod:international-poverty-line) of $3 per day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
note: Poverty data is expressed in [international-$](#dod:int_dollar_abbreviation) at 2021 prices. Depending on the country and year, it relates to income (measured after taxes and benefits) or to consumption, [per capita](#dod:per-capita).
caption:
Cereal yield vs. share in extreme poverty

Extreme poverty is defined as living below the International Poverty Line of $3 per day. This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This line chart tracks two distinct metrics for Sub-Saharan Africa from 1990 through 2026. The vertical axis measures percentage change, ranging from -30% to +60%, while the horizontal axis marks time in five-year increments. The blue line, representing cereal yield in kilograms per hectare, begins at the 0% baseline in 1990 and follows a generally upward trajectory. It experiences a sharp initial dip below -10% around 1992 before recovering. From the mid-1990s onward, the line climbs steadily, crossing the +30% mark around 2010 and reaching a peak slightly above +50% near 2020, followed by a minor plateau. In contrast, the orange line, indicating the share of the population in extreme poverty, also starts at 0% in 1990 but trends downward. After a brief period of fluctuation near the baseline until roughly 2000, the line descends consistently. It drops below -10% around 2005, passes -20% by 2010, and continues to fall, ending near the -30% mark by 2026. The poverty data is expressed in international dollars at 2021 prices and relates to income or consumption per capita depending on the specific country and year. Visually, the two lines diverge significantly over the thirty-six-year period, with cereal yields rising into positive percentage territory while the share of the population in extreme poverty decreases into negative percentage territory relative to the 1990 baseline.
